questions.theory: Combining a particular premise with a universal can yield a particular conclusion. If some A are B and all B are C, then some A are C.

questions.question: Researchers note that ice cream sales and drowning incidents both rise in the same months. What is the best explanation?
options: The data must be wrong.
Drowning causes ice cream sales.
Ice cream causes drowning.
A third factor, hot weather, raises both.
questions.explanation: Hot summer weather independently increases both ice cream sales and swimming, explaining the shared pattern without direct causation.
questions.theory: When two variables correlate, a common cause may drive both. Identifying a lurking variable explains the link without one event causing the other.

questions.question: Which fact would most strengthen the claim that wearing a helmet reduces head injuries among cyclists?
options: Helmets are required by law in some cities.
Many cyclists own helmets.
Cyclists with helmets had far fewer head injuries than similar cyclists without them.
Helmets come in many colors.
questions.explanation: A comparison showing fewer head injuries among helmet wearers than comparable non-wearers directly supports the protective claim.
questions.theory: Strengthening evidence shows the effect varies with the presence of the proposed cause. Comparing similar groups isolates the factor of interest.

questions.question: All squares are rectangles. Figure X is not a rectangle. What follows?
options: Figure X is not a square.
Figure X is a triangle.
Figure X is a square.
All rectangles are squares.
questions.explanation: Since every square is a rectangle and X is not a rectangle, X cannot be a square.
questions.theory: Modus tollens applied to a category: if all A are B and something is not B, it is not A. The absence of the broader property rules out the narrower class.

questions.question: Argument: 'Lena will be a great pilot. She has wanted to fly since childhood.' What does the argument assume?
options: Pilots earn high salaries.
Long-standing desire to fly leads to being a great pilot.
Lena owns a plane.
Lena has flown before.
questions.explanation: The conclusion needs the assumption that wanting to fly since childhood translates into being a great pilot.
questions.theory: An assumption connects given evidence to the conclusion. The argument presumes desire reliably produces skill, which must hold for the reasoning to work.

questions.question: If the store has a sale, prices are reduced. Prices are reduced. Which conclusion is NOT valid?
options: The store definitely has a sale.
The store may have a sale.
Prices are reduced now.
Reduced prices are observed.
questions.explanation: Concluding the store definitely has a sale from reduced prices is affirming the consequent, since prices could drop for other reasons.
questions.theory: Affirming the consequent is invalid because the consequent may have multiple causes. A true 'then' does not guarantee the specific 'if.'

questions.question: Either the file was deleted or it was moved. The file was not deleted. What follows?
options: The file was moved.
The file was copied.
The file was deleted.
The file is corrupted.
questions.explanation: With deletion ruled out, disjunctive syllogism leaves 'moved' as the true option.
questions.theory: Disjunctive syllogism: 'P or Q' plus 'not P' yields Q. Eliminating one alternative confirms the other.

questions.question: All birds have feathers. A penguin is a bird. Which conclusion must be true?
options: Penguins are not birds.
A penguin can fly.
All feathered things are penguins.
A penguin has feathers.
questions.explanation: Since all birds have feathers and a penguin is a bird, a penguin has feathers.
questions.theory: Universal instantiation: a property held by all members of a class applies to any specific member. The shared category transfers the trait.

questions.question: Consider: 'If the meeting is canceled, I will go home. The meeting was canceled.' It validly follows that I will go home.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: The antecedent is affirmed, so by modus ponens the consequent 'I will go home' follows.
questions.theory: Modus ponens confirms the consequent when the antecedent is given. The inference preserves truth from premises to conclusion.

questions.question: A town added more streetlights and crime fell. A council member says the lights caused the drop. Which fact most weakens this?
options: Police also doubled patrols in the same area at the same time.
The streetlights were expensive.
The lights were bright and white.
Residents liked the new lights.
questions.explanation: Doubled patrols offer an alternative cause for the crime drop, weakening the claim that lights were responsible.
questions.theory: A causal argument is weakened by a competing cause acting at the same time. If another factor could explain the effect, the original cause is uncertain.

questions.question: No fish can breathe air indefinitely. A trout is a fish. What follows?
options: All air-breathers are fish.
A trout can breathe air indefinitely.
A trout is not a fish.
A trout cannot breathe air indefinitely.
questions.explanation: The universal negative applies to the trout as a fish, so it cannot breathe air indefinitely.
questions.theory: A 'no A can B' statement applies to every member of A. Instantiating it to one member yields a negative claim about that member.

questions.question: Argument: 'The bridge is safe because engineers inspected it last week.' Which is a premise?
options: The bridge should be closed.
Drivers trust the bridge.
Engineers inspected it last week.
The bridge is safe.
questions.explanation: The inspection is the supporting reason, making it the premise, while 'the bridge is safe' is the conclusion.
questions.theory: A premise provides support; the conclusion is what it supports. The clause following 'because' typically states the premise.

questions.question: If a card is red, then it is not a club. This card is a club. What follows?
options: The card is red.
Nothing can be concluded.
The card is not red.
The card is a heart.
questions.explanation: Being a club denies the consequent 'not a club,' so by modus tollens the antecedent 'red' is false.
questions.theory: Modus tollens works even when the consequent is itself a negation. Denying the consequent forces denial of the antecedent.

questions.question: Which fact, if true, most strengthens the claim that a daily walk improves mood?
options: People randomly assigned to daily walks reported better moods than those not assigned.
Parks are pleasant places.
Walking is a popular activity.
Walking shoes are widely sold.
questions.explanation: A randomized comparison showing better mood among walkers directly supports that walking improves mood.
questions.theory: Random assignment helps isolate the proposed cause by balancing other factors. A resulting difference strengthens the causal claim.

questions.question: Some books in this library are rare. All rare books are valuable. Which conclusion is valid?
options: All books here are valuable.
All valuable books are here.
No books here are valuable.
Some books here are valuable.
questions.explanation: The rare books here are valuable, so at least some books here are valuable.
questions.theory: A particular premise plus a universal yields a particular conclusion. If some A are B and all B are C, then some A are C.

questions.question: If the password is correct, access is granted. Access was not granted. What follows?
options: The system crashed.
The password is not correct.
The password is correct.
Access was granted anyway.
questions.explanation: No access denies the consequent, so by modus tollens the password is not correct.
questions.theory: Modus tollens: not Q implies not P given 'if P then Q.' The failure of the result rules out the condition.

questions.question: Consider: 'All planets orbit the sun in this system. Mars is a planet in this system.' It validly follows that Mars orbits the sun.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Applying the universal to Mars as a planet in the system yields that Mars orbits the sun.
questions.theory: Universal instantiation transfers a 'for all' property to any named member. The conclusion is guaranteed when the member belongs to the class.

questions.question: A blogger writes: 'Cities with more bookstores have higher literacy, so opening bookstores raises literacy.' What is the reasoning flaw?
options: The blogger treats a correlation as if it proves causation.
The blogger dislikes bookstores.
The blogger ignored libraries entirely.
The blogger used too few cities.
questions.explanation: The argument leaps from an association between bookstores and literacy to a causal claim without ruling out other factors.
questions.theory: Inferring causation from correlation is a classic error. The shared trend may stem from wealth, education funding, or another underlying cause.

questions.question: All triangles have three sides. This shape does not have three sides. What follows?
options: This shape is a square.
This shape is a triangle.
This shape is not a triangle.
All three-sided shapes are triangles.
questions.explanation: Lacking three sides denies the necessary property, so the shape cannot be a triangle by modus tollens.
questions.theory: If membership requires a property and that property is absent, membership is denied. This is modus tollens over a universal claim.

questions.question: Argument: 'Our team will win the championship because we won our last three games.' What does this assume?
options: The team practices daily.
Other teams are weak.
Recent wins reliably predict winning the championship.
The championship is important.
questions.explanation: The conclusion depends on assuming that a recent winning streak reliably predicts a championship.
questions.theory: An assumption is the unstated link making an argument work. Here it is that past short-term results forecast the final outcome.

questions.question: Either the keys are in the car or on the table. The keys are not on the table. What follows?
options: The keys are lost.
The keys are on the table.
The keys are in the car.
The keys are in a pocket.
questions.explanation: With the table ruled out, disjunctive syllogism leaves 'in the car' as the true option.
questions.theory: Disjunctive syllogism: 'P or Q' and 'not Q' give P. Eliminating one location confirms the other.

questions.question: If a shape is a square, then it has four equal sides. This shape has four equal sides. Which conclusion is invalid?
options: This shape has four equal sides.
This shape might be a square.
Four equal sides are present.
This shape is certainly a square.
questions.explanation: Concluding the shape is certainly a square from four equal sides affirms the consequent, since a rhombus also fits.
questions.theory: Affirming the consequent fails because the consequent can be met without the antecedent. Four equal sides do not guarantee a square.

questions.question: All employees here must wear a badge. Dana is not wearing a badge. Assuming the rule is followed and observed, what follows?
options: Dana forgot her badge.
Dana is not an employee here.
Dana is an employee here.
All badge-wearers are employees.
questions.explanation: Since every employee wears a badge and Dana has none, Dana is not an employee here.
questions.theory: Modus tollens over a universal: if all A do B and someone does not do B, that person is not an A.

questions.question: A manager observes that employees who take more breaks are more productive and concludes breaks boost productivity. Which fact most weakens this?
options: Breaks are taken in a lounge.
Breaks last fifteen minutes each.
Employees enjoy their breaks.
The most productive employees finish early, which gives them time to take more breaks.
questions.explanation: If high productivity leads to more free time for breaks, the causal direction is reversed, weakening the claim that breaks cause productivity.
questions.theory: Reversed causation weakens a causal claim: the supposed effect may actually drive the supposed cause. Establishing direction is essential.

questions.question: No mammals are insects. All bees are insects. What follows?
options: No bee is a mammal.
Some bees are mammals.
All insects are bees.
All bees are mammals.
questions.explanation: Bees lie entirely within insects, which share nothing with mammals, so no bee is a mammal.
questions.theory: Combining 'all A are B' with 'no B are C' validly gives 'no A are C.' The categories A and C cannot overlap.

questions.question: Argument: 'Since the lights are off, no one is home.' Which is the unstated assumption?
options: People dislike darkness.
If someone were home, the lights would be on.
The lights use electricity.
The house has many lights.
questions.explanation: The argument assumes that anyone home would have the lights on, linking 'lights off' to 'no one home.'
questions.theory: An assumption bridges premise and conclusion. Here it supplies the conditional connecting the observation about lights to the claim about occupancy.

questions.question: If a student is enrolled, they have an ID number. Priya has an ID number. What can we conclude?
options: Having an ID number does not by itself prove Priya is enrolled.
No enrolled student has an ID number.
Priya is definitely enrolled.
Priya is definitely not enrolled.
questions.explanation: An ID number is necessary but the conditional does not say it is exclusive to enrollment, so it alone does not prove enrollment.
questions.theory: Affirming the consequent is invalid: the consequent may hold for reasons other than the antecedent. A necessary condition is not automatically sufficient.

questions.question: A city installed brighter streetlights on Oak Avenue, and over the next year reported traffic accidents on that street fell by 30 percent. The city concluded the brighter lights caused the drop in accidents. Which of the following, if true, most weakens this conclusion?
options: Oak Avenue had more accidents than any other street before the lights were installed.
During the same year, the city lowered the speed limit on Oak Avenue and added speed cameras.
Drivers reported that the brighter lights made the street feel safer at night.
The new streetlights were more expensive to maintain than the old ones.
questions.explanation: A simultaneous speed-limit reduction and cameras provide an alternative cause for the accident drop, undermining the claim that the lights were responsible.
questions.theory: A causal claim is weakened when a confounding variable changes at the same time as the proposed cause, since the observed effect could be due to that other factor rather than the one credited.

questions.question: A study found that employees who eat breakfast at the company cafeteria are promoted more often than those who skip breakfast. The author concludes that eating breakfast improves job performance and thus promotion prospects. The argument depends on which assumption?
options: Most employees at the company eat breakfast.
Promotions at the company are based primarily on job performance.
The cafeteria serves nutritious breakfast options.
Eating breakfast, rather than some trait shared by breakfast-eaters, is what accounts for the difference in promotions.
questions.explanation: The conclusion assumes the breakfast itself, not a confounding trait common to breakfast-eaters, explains the promotion gap.
questions.theory: A necessary assumption rules out an alternative explanation; here the argument must assume no shared characteristic of breakfast-eaters (such as discipline) drives both the breakfast habit and the promotions.

questions.question: Researchers observed that people who own more books tend to score higher on reading comprehension tests. A commentator argued that buying more books therefore improves reading comprehension. Which alternative explanation most plausibly accounts for the correlation without buying books being the cause?
options: Some people own books purely for decoration and never read them.
Bookstores often run promotions that encourage bulk purchases.
People who already read well enjoy reading and therefore choose to buy more books.
Reading comprehension tests are sometimes scored inconsistently.
questions.explanation: Strong existing reading ability could cause both book-buying and high test scores, reversing the assumed direction of causation.
questions.theory: Reverse causation occurs when the supposed effect is actually the cause; a pre-existing trait can independently produce both correlated variables.

questions.question: A nutrition columnist wrote: 'In a survey of marathon runners, those who drank a particular sports drink reported fewer leg cramps. Therefore, this sports drink prevents leg cramps in the general population.' Which of the following best identifies a flaw in this reasoning?
options: The columnist fails to define what counts as a leg cramp.
Marathon runners may not be representative of the general population.
The survey did not measure how the drink tastes to participants.
Some runners may have consumed other beverages as well.
questions.explanation: Generalizing from marathon runners to everyone is unjustified because the sampled group is atypical of the broader population.
questions.theory: Sampling bias arises when a conclusion about a broad population is drawn from a sample that is not representative of that population.

questions.question: A manager claims that the company's new wellness program reduced sick days, citing that employees who enrolled took 40 percent fewer sick days than non-enrollees. Which of the following, if true, most undermines the manager's claim?
options: Enrollment in the program was voluntary, and healthier employees were far more likely to enroll.
The program included gym memberships and nutrition counseling.
The wellness program was advertised heavily through company email.
Non-enrollees were given the option to join the program later.
questions.explanation: Voluntary enrollment by already-healthier employees means the difference in sick days could reflect who chose to join rather than any effect of the program.
questions.theory: Self-selection bias occurs when participants choose whether to be in a group, so pre-existing differences between the groups, not the intervention, may explain the outcome.

questions.question: Editorial: 'Towns that have more police officers per capita also report more crimes. Clearly, hiring more police officers leads to more crime, so towns should reduce their police forces.' Which of the following best describes the error in this argument?
options: It fails to consider the cost of hiring additional officers.
It treats a correlation as evidence of a causal relationship while ignoring that high crime may lead towns to hire more police.
It relies on statistics from an unnamed source.
It assumes that all towns have the same population size.
questions.explanation: The editorial overlooks that high crime rates likely cause towns to hire more police, reversing the claimed causal direction.
questions.theory: Reverse causation is a common flaw in correlation-to-causation arguments; the proposed effect may actually be driving the proposed cause.

questions.question: A scientist argues that a new fertilizer increases crop yield because fields treated with it produced 20 percent more grain than untreated fields last season. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the argument?
options: Farmers who used the fertilizer were satisfied with the results.
The fertilizer is cheaper than competing products.
The treated and untreated fields had similar soil quality, rainfall, and sunlight last season.
The scientist has studied fertilizers for over twenty years.
questions.explanation: Showing the fields were similar in other growing conditions rules out confounders, supporting that the fertilizer caused the yield difference.
questions.theory: A causal argument is strengthened by evidence that the compared groups were alike in all relevant respects except the proposed cause, isolating that cause as the explanation.

questions.question: A health blog states: 'People who take daily vitamin supplements live longer than those who don't. So everyone should take daily supplements to extend their lifespan.' Which assumption is required for this conclusion to hold?
options: All vitamin supplements contain the same ingredients.
Doctors generally recommend vitamin supplements.
People who take supplements do not also differ in other health-related behaviors that affect lifespan.
Vitamin supplements are affordable for most people.
questions.explanation: The argument must assume supplement-takers don't differ in other lifespan-affecting habits, or those habits, not the supplements, could explain the longevity.
questions.theory: A necessary assumption in a causal generalization is that no confounding behavioral difference between the groups accounts for the observed effect.

questions.question: A company found that departments using its new project-management software completed projects faster than departments not using it. Before concluding the software caused the speedup, which question would be most useful to answer?
options: Were the faster departments already more efficient before adopting the software?
Does the software have a mobile version?
How much did the software license cost per department?
How many employees work in each department?
questions.explanation: If the faster departments were already more efficient, their pre-existing efficiency rather than the software could explain the difference.
questions.theory: To test a causal claim, one checks whether a baseline difference existed before the intervention, since a pre-existing advantage would undermine attributing the outcome to the intervention.

questions.question: Advertisement: 'Four out of five dentists surveyed recommend Brand X toothpaste. Therefore, Brand X is the most effective toothpaste available.' Which of the following best identifies the reasoning flaw?
options: Most people do not visit the dentist regularly.
Toothpaste effectiveness cannot be measured at all.
The survey may have only included dentists already affiliated with Brand X.
The advertisement does not mention the price of Brand X.
questions.explanation: If the surveyed dentists were not randomly selected but tied to Brand X, the recommendation reflects bias rather than effectiveness.
questions.theory: A biased or non-representative sample undermines a conclusion; results from a self-interested or hand-picked group cannot support a claim about overall quality.

questions.question: Consider this argument: 'Children who attend preschool perform better in first grade than children who do not. Therefore, preschool attendance causes better first-grade performance.' If it turns out that families who enroll children in preschool also tend to read to their children more often at home, this fact weakens the argument. Is this statement true or false?
options: True
False
questions.explanation: The home-reading difference is a confounder that could explain the better performance, so it does weaken the causal claim about preschool.
questions.theory: A confounding variable correlated with both the cause and the effect undermines a causal conclusion by offering an alternative explanation for the observed difference.

questions.question: A researcher claims that drinking coffee reduces the risk of a certain disease, citing that coffee drinkers in a survey had lower disease rates. A critic counters that coffee drinkers in the survey were also far more likely to be non-smokers. This counterargument suggests that the original claim suffers from which problem?
options: A reliance on outdated survey data.
A confounding factor that may explain the lower disease rate.
A sample that is too small to be reliable.
An ambiguous definition of what counts as coffee.
questions.explanation: Non-smoking, correlated with coffee drinking, could be the true cause of the lower disease rate, making it a confounder.
questions.theory: A confounder is a third variable associated with both the supposed cause and the effect; its presence means the correlation may not reflect the claimed causal link.

questions.question: Politician: 'Since I took office two years ago, the city's unemployment rate has dropped sharply. My economic policies are clearly responsible for this improvement.' Which of the following, if true, most weakens the politician's claim?
options: The city's population grew during the same period.
The politician campaigned heavily on economic issues.
The politician's policies were popular with local business owners.
Unemployment dropped by similar amounts in neighboring cities that did not adopt the politician's policies.
questions.explanation: Similar drops in cities without the policies suggest a regional trend caused the improvement, not the politician's actions.
questions.theory: A causal claim is weakened when the same effect appears in comparable cases lacking the proposed cause, pointing to a broader common factor.

questions.question: An analyst observes that companies with on-site daycare have higher employee retention. She concludes that adding on-site daycare will improve retention at any company. Which of the following most calls her conclusion into question?
options: Companies that already had strong, employee-friendly cultures were the ones that chose to add on-site daycare.
Some employees do not have young children.
Retention can also be measured by employee surveys.
On-site daycare is expensive to operate.
questions.explanation: If employee-friendly companies both add daycare and retain workers, the underlying culture, not the daycare, may explain high retention.
questions.theory: When the entities adopting a practice differ systematically from those that don't, that pre-existing difference may be the real cause of the outcome, not the practice.

questions.question: Argument: 'A recent study showed that towns with more farmers' markets have lower obesity rates. Building more farmers' markets is therefore an effective way to reduce obesity.' Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen this argument?
options: Farmers' markets sell produce at competitive prices.
When several towns added farmers' markets, their obesity rates subsequently declined relative to similar towns that added none.
Farmers' markets support local agriculture.
Many people enjoy shopping at farmers' markets.
questions.explanation: Showing obesity declined after markets were added, versus comparable towns without them, supports a causal effect rather than mere correlation.
questions.theory: A correlation-based causal claim is strengthened by evidence that introducing the cause is followed by the effect, especially when compared against a control group lacking the cause.

questions.question: A teacher notes that students who sit in the front rows of her class earn higher grades than those in the back. She plans to assign struggling students to the front to raise their grades. Which of the following best explains why her plan might not work?
options: The classroom may not have enough front-row seats.
The students who chose front-row seats may already be more motivated, and seating may not affect grades.
Front-row seats may be uncomfortable for some students.
Grades depend partly on homework completed outside class.
questions.explanation: If motivation, not seat location, drives the grade difference, reassigning seats won't help struggling students.
questions.theory: Self-selection means those who chose a condition may differ in relevant traits; forcing others into that condition need not reproduce the outcome if the trait, not the condition, caused it.

questions.question: Columnist: 'Studies show that married people are happier on average than unmarried people. Therefore, getting married will make an unhappy person happier.' Which of the following is an assumption on which this argument depends?
options: Marriage ceremonies are meaningful to most couples.
Happier people are not simply more likely to get and stay married in the first place.
Unmarried people want to be happier.
Most people get married at some point in their lives.
questions.explanation: The argument assumes marriage causes happiness rather than happiness leading people to marry, which it must rule out.
questions.theory: Guarding against reverse causation is a necessary assumption: the argument must assume the effect (happiness) does not cause the supposed cause (marriage).

questions.question: A manufacturer claims its motor oil reduces engine wear, citing that engines using the oil lasted longer in a test. Critics note that the test engines using the new oil were also brand new, while the comparison engines were several years old. This criticism points out that the test failed to:
options: Measure the exact amount of wear in each engine.
Control for the initial condition of the engines being compared.
Account for differences in driving conditions.
Use a large enough number of engines.
questions.explanation: Because the new-oil engines started newer, their longer life may reflect their initial condition, not the oil; the test didn't control this variable.
questions.theory: A valid comparison requires that groups differ only in the variable being tested; uncontrolled baseline differences confound the result.

questions.question: Survey result: 'When customers leaving our luxury store were asked, 90 percent said they were satisfied with their purchase. This proves that 90 percent of all consumers are satisfied with our brand.' Which of the following best identifies the flaw?
options: The survey did not ask customers about specific products.
The store should have surveyed a larger number of customers.
Customers who chose to leave with a purchase are not representative of all consumers.
Satisfaction is difficult to measure accurately.
questions.explanation: Surveying only purchasing customers excludes those who left without buying or never visited, so the sample can't represent all consumers.
questions.theory: Survivorship and selection bias occur when a sample is drawn only from those who completed a process, omitting those who opted out and skewing the result.

questions.question: A study reports that neighborhoods with more trees have lower rates of anxiety among residents. A journalist concludes that planting trees reduces anxiety. Which of the following, if true, most weakens this conclusion?
options: Trees provide shade and improve air quality.
Some residents are allergic to certain types of trees.
Wealthier neighborhoods tend to have both more trees and residents with greater access to mental health care.
The study measured anxiety using a standard questionnaire.
questions.explanation: Wealth, linked to both more trees and better mental health care, is a confounder that could explain the lower anxiety apart from the trees.
questions.theory: A lurking variable correlated with both factors weakens a causal claim by offering an alternative cause for the association.

questions.question: Manager: 'Employees who use our optional mentorship program get promoted faster, so the program accelerates careers. We should expand it.' Which of the following would be most useful to determine before expanding the program?
options: Whether employees who enroll in the program are already more ambitious or higher-performing than those who don't.
How many mentors are currently available.
Whether the program is popular among new hires.
How long the average mentorship session lasts.
questions.explanation: If enrollees are already more ambitious or higher-performing, that trait, not the program, may explain faster promotions.
questions.theory: Optional participation invites self-selection; verifying whether participants differ at baseline is essential before crediting the program with the outcome.

questions.question: Argument: 'Cities that banned plastic bags saw a decrease in litter the following year. The bans must have caused the decrease.' Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen this causal claim?
options: In the year before the bans, litter levels in those cities had been stable, and no other anti-litter measures were introduced alongside the bans.
Residents of those cities supported the bans.
Plastic bags are made from non-renewable resources.
Reusable bags are more durable than plastic bags.
questions.explanation: Stable prior litter levels and the absence of other measures rule out alternative causes, supporting that the ban produced the decrease.
questions.theory: Ruling out competing explanations and establishing a stable baseline before the intervention strengthens the inference that the intervention caused the change.

questions.question: A college reports that students who study abroad earn higher starting salaries after graduation than those who don't. The college advertises study abroad as a way to boost future earnings. Which of the following, if true, most undermines this advertisement?
options: Some employers value foreign language skills.
Studying abroad is expensive, so the students who do it tend to come from wealthier, well-connected families.
Study-abroad programs expose students to new cultures.
Not all majors offer study-abroad opportunities.
questions.explanation: If study-abroad students come from wealthier, well-connected families, those advantages, not the program, may produce higher salaries.
questions.theory: When access to an intervention is tied to pre-existing advantages, those advantages confound the outcome and weaken claims that the intervention is responsible.

questions.question: Researcher: 'In our data, ice cream sales and drowning deaths rise and fall together across the year. We hypothesize that eating ice cream impairs swimming ability, causing drownings.' Which of the following best explains the correlation without supporting the researcher's hypothesis?
options: Hot summer weather independently increases both ice cream sales and swimming, and thus drownings.
People sometimes swim shortly after eating.
Ice cream contains sugar, which can affect energy levels.
Drowning deaths are tragic and underreported.
questions.explanation: Warm weather is a common cause that raises both ice cream sales and swimming activity, explaining the correlation without any direct link.
questions.theory: A spurious correlation arises when a third variable independently influences two others; the two then move together without one causing the other.

questions.question: An online retailer finds that customers who receive its promotional emails spend more than customers who do not. The retailer concludes the emails cause higher spending. If customers had to opt in to receive promotional emails, and the most enthusiastic customers were the ones who opted in, then this fact weakens the retailer's conclusion. Is this statement true or false?
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Opt-in by the most enthusiastic customers means their pre-existing enthusiasm, not the emails, could drive the higher spending, weakening the claim.
questions.theory: Self-selection through opt-in creates groups that differ before any treatment, so the treatment cannot be credited with differences that the selection itself may explain.

questions.question: A hospital administrator notes that patients treated in the intensive care unit have a higher death rate than patients in general wards. He concludes that the intensive care unit provides worse care. Which of the following best identifies the flaw in his reasoning?
options: Patients sent to intensive care are typically far sicker to begin with than those in general wards.
The hospital may be understaffed in both units.
Death rates can fluctuate from month to month.
He does not specify how care quality is measured.
questions.explanation: More severely ill patients are placed in intensive care, so their higher death rate reflects their condition, not inferior care.
questions.theory: Selection of a group based on severity confounds outcome comparisons; the group's worse baseline, not the treatment, can explain a worse outcome.

questions.question: Argument: 'Our new training seminar works. We surveyed graduates of the seminar, and 85 percent said it improved their public-speaking skills.' Which of the following, if true, most weakens this argument?
options: The seminar lasts only one day.
Public speaking is a skill valued by many employers.
The seminar covers both speech writing and delivery.
People who feel a seminar wasted their time are far less likely to respond to follow-up surveys.
questions.explanation: If dissatisfied graduates rarely respond, the survey overrepresents satisfied ones, inflating the apparent success rate.
questions.theory: Non-response bias skews results when those with negative experiences are less likely to participate, making the responding sample unrepresentative.

questions.question: A wellness study concludes that yoga reduces blood pressure because participants who did yoga for three months had lower blood pressure at the end than at the start. Which of the following, if true, most weakens this conclusion?
options: During the same three months, participants also started a low-sodium diet recommended by the study organizers.
Yoga has been practiced for thousands of years.
Participants enjoyed the yoga sessions.
Blood pressure was measured by trained nurses.
questions.explanation: A simultaneous low-sodium diet is an alternative cause of the blood-pressure drop, so yoga cannot be credited for it.
questions.theory: Without a control group, any co-occurring change can confound a before-and-after comparison, undermining attribution to the intended cause.

questions.question: Economist: 'Countries that export more tend to have stronger economies. Therefore, if a struggling country increases its exports, its economy will strengthen.' Which assumption does this argument require?
options: Exports are easier to measure than imports.
Most countries participate in international trade.
A strong economy does not itself enable a country to export more, rather than the other way around.
Exporting goods is profitable for most companies.
questions.explanation: The argument must assume exports drive economic strength rather than a strong economy enabling more exports, ruling out reverse causation.
questions.theory: A necessary assumption in directing policy from a correlation is that the causal arrow points the assumed way and not the reverse.

questions.question: A school district observes that schools with more computers per student have higher test scores. The district plans to buy more computers to raise scores. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the plan's underlying assumption?
options: Computers are becoming less expensive over time.
Teachers in the district are comfortable with technology.
In a controlled trial, schools randomly given additional computers later showed test-score gains over schools that received none.
Students generally enjoy using computers.
questions.explanation: A randomized trial showing gains from added computers supports that computers cause higher scores, validating the plan.
questions.theory: Randomized assignment removes self-selection and confounders, so an effect observed under randomization strongly supports a causal interpretation.

questions.question: Claim: 'A survey of our website visitors found that 95 percent prefer online shopping to shopping in physical stores. This shows most consumers now prefer shopping online.' Which of the following best identifies the reasoning error?
options: Online and in-store prices may differ.
The survey did not ask about specific product categories.
People who visit a shopping website are more likely to favor online shopping than the general public.
The survey sample of visitors was probably too small.
questions.explanation: Website visitors are predisposed to favor online shopping, so their preferences don't represent all consumers.
questions.theory: Selection bias from the venue of sampling skews results; surveying a group already inclined toward the measured preference inflates that preference.

questions.question: A pundit argues: 'Regions with higher chocolate consumption have produced more Nobel laureates per capita. Eating chocolate must enhance the kind of thinking that wins Nobel Prizes.' Which of the following, if true, most weakens this argument?
options: Nobel Prizes are awarded in several different fields.
Chocolate contains compounds that some studies link to mood.
Wealthier regions tend to have both higher chocolate consumption and better-funded research institutions.
Chocolate consumption has increased worldwide over time.
questions.explanation: Regional wealth, tied to both chocolate consumption and research funding, is a confounder that better explains the Nobel correlation.
questions.theory: When a third variable plausibly causes both correlated quantities, the original causal explanation is undermined.

questions.question: A fitness app reports that users who log their meals lose more weight than users who don't. The company concludes meal-logging causes weight loss and urges all users to log meals. Which of the following would most help evaluate whether the conclusion is justified?
options: Whether logging meals takes more than a few minutes per day.
Whether the meal-logging feature is easy to use.
Whether the app sends reminders to log meals.
Whether users who choose to log meals are also more committed to their weight-loss goals overall.
questions.explanation: If meal-loggers are simply more committed, their commitment, not the logging, could explain the greater weight loss.
questions.theory: Voluntary behaviors signal underlying motivation; distinguishing the behavior's effect from the motivation behind it is key to a sound causal judgment.

questions.question: Argument: 'In a clinical trial, patients given the new drug recovered faster than those given a placebo. The patients were randomly assigned to the two groups, and neither patients nor doctors knew who received which. Therefore, the drug speeds recovery.' Which of the following, if true, would most weaken this conclusion?
options: The trial was funded by a government health agency.
The placebo pills looked identical to the drug pills.
The drug is more expensive than existing treatments.
Far more patients in the drug group than in the placebo group dropped out of the trial before it ended, and only those who recovered quickly remained.
questions.explanation: Differential dropout leaving only fast recoverers in the drug group biases the comparison despite the randomization.
questions.theory: Even a randomized, blinded trial can be undermined by attrition bias if dropouts differ systematically between groups, distorting the remaining sample.

questions.question: A city official notes that more umbrellas are seen on the street on days when it rains, and concludes that carrying umbrellas causes rain. Which of the following best describes the flaw in this reasoning?
options: The official failed to consider days with no precipitation.
The official did not distinguish between types of umbrellas.
The official reversed the causal direction; rain causes people to carry umbrellas.
The official used an unreliable method to count umbrellas.
questions.explanation: Rain prompts umbrella use, so the official has the causal direction backward.
questions.theory: Reverse causation mislabels the effect as the cause; recognizing which event naturally precedes and prompts the other corrects the error.

questions.question: A study finds that employees who telecommute report higher job satisfaction than those who work in the office. The author concludes that telecommuting raises job satisfaction. Which of the following, if true, most weakens this conclusion?
options: Some telecommuters work irregular hours.
Telecommuters save money on commuting costs.
Only employees who already had high satisfaction and strong performance records were allowed to telecommute.
Office workers have access to more in-person collaboration.
questions.explanation: If only already-satisfied, high-performing employees were permitted to telecommute, their prior satisfaction, not telecommuting, explains the difference.
questions.theory: When eligibility for a condition depends on a trait correlated with the outcome, that trait confounds the comparison and weakens the causal claim.

questions.question: Advocate: 'Schools that adopted our anti-bullying curriculum reported fewer bullying incidents the next year. The curriculum clearly reduces bullying.' Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen this claim?
options: Teachers found the curriculum easy to deliver.
The curriculum was developed by child psychologists.
Schools that adopted the curriculum and otherwise comparable schools that did not were tracked, and only the adopting schools saw incident declines.
The curriculum includes role-playing exercises.
questions.explanation: Comparing adopters with similar non-adopters and finding declines only among adopters supports that the curriculum caused the reduction.
questions.theory: A controlled comparison isolating the intervention strengthens causal inference by showing the effect tracks the presence of the cause.

questions.question: A report claims that listening to classical music makes infants smarter, citing that infants who were played classical music scored higher on a developmental test. Which of the following assumptions is necessary for this claim?
options: Classical music is widely available to parents.
Parents who play classical music for infants do not also provide other forms of stimulation that affect development.
Infants can perceive differences between musical genres.
Developmental tests are administered by professionals.
questions.explanation: The claim must assume these parents don't provide other developmental stimulation, or that stimulation, not the music, could explain the scores.
questions.theory: A necessary assumption excludes parallel causes; the argument depends on no other enriching behavior accompanying the proposed cause.

questions.question: A bank finds that customers who use its mobile app have higher account balances than customers who don't. It concludes that using the app encourages saving. Which of the following, if true, most undermines this conclusion?
options: Some customers use both the app and the website.
The app allows users to transfer money between accounts.
The app is free to download.
Customers with higher balances and more complex finances are the ones most likely to adopt the app.
questions.explanation: If wealthier customers are the ones adopting the app, their existing balances, not the app, account for the difference.
questions.theory: Reverse causation or self-selection by pre-existing status undermines a causal claim when the trait drives both adoption and the measured outcome.

questions.question: Argument: 'Our employee referral program is effective: referred hires stay at the company longer than non-referred hires.' Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the claim that the referral process itself causes longer tenure?
options: Referred hires are often recommended by long-tenured employees.
Referred candidates tend to be screened more carefully by the referring employee before applying, so weaker candidates are filtered out early.
Some referred hires work in different departments than their referrers.
The referral program offers a cash bonus to referrers.
questions.explanation: If referrers pre-filter out weaker candidates, the surviving referred hires differ in quality, so screening, not the referral process per se, may explain longer tenure.
questions.theory: A selection mechanism that alters who enters a group can produce outcome differences independent of the process being credited.

questions.question: A researcher reports that students who participate in extracurricular sports have higher grade-point averages than non-participants and concludes that playing sports improves academic performance. Suppose schools require a minimum grade-point average to remain eligible for sports. This eligibility rule, if true, weakens the researcher's causal conclusion. Is this statement true or false?
options: True
False
questions.explanation: An eligibility rule means only students who already have high grades can play, so high grades enable sports rather than sports causing high grades, weakening the claim.
questions.theory: Reverse causation is exposed when the supposed effect is actually a precondition for the supposed cause, here grades gating sports participation.

questions.question: A diet company claims its program works because customers who completed the full 12-week program lost significant weight. Which of the following, if true, most weakens this claim?
options: The program is more affordable than some competitors.
The program includes weekly coaching calls.
Customers who were not losing weight tended to quit the program before the 12 weeks ended.
Weight was measured on the company's own scales.
questions.explanation: If those not losing weight dropped out, only successful customers remained, making the completers a biased sample that overstates effectiveness.
questions.theory: Attrition or survivorship bias inflates apparent success when failures leave the sample before being counted.

questions.question: A commentator argues that violent video games cause aggression, citing a survey in which teens who play violent games reported more aggressive feelings. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the causal claim?
options: Many violent games carry mature-content ratings.
In an experiment, teens randomly assigned to play violent games afterward showed more aggression than teens randomly assigned to play non-violent games.
Aggressive feelings are common during adolescence.
Violent video games are popular among teenagers.
questions.explanation: Random assignment to violent versus non-violent games, with the violent group more aggressive afterward, supports causation by ruling out self-selection.
questions.theory: Randomized experiments support causal claims because randomization balances pre-existing traits, so a post-treatment difference is attributable to the treatment.

questions.question: A study finds that people who sleep with the bedroom light on are more likely to be nearsighted. Researchers concluded that sleeping with the light on causes nearsightedness. Later, critics noted that nearsighted parents are more likely both to be nearsighted themselves (a heritable trait) and to leave a light on for their children. This criticism suggests the original study overlooked:
options: Whether the children wore glasses.
The age at which children develop nearsightedness.
A common factor, parental nearsightedness, influencing both the light habit and the children's eyesight.
The brightness level of the lights used.
questions.explanation: Parental nearsightedness could cause both the light-leaving habit and the children's inherited nearsightedness, making it a confounder.
questions.theory: A common cause affecting both variables creates a misleading correlation; failing to account for it can produce a false causal conclusion.

questions.question: Argument: 'A poll conducted by calling landline telephone numbers during weekday afternoons found that most respondents favor increasing funding for senior centers. This shows the public broadly supports such funding.' Which of the following best identifies the weakness in this argument?
options: People reachable by landline on weekday afternoons skew older and may not represent the general public.
Senior centers provide valuable community services.
The poll should have been conducted over several weeks.
The poll did not ask how much funding should increase.
questions.explanation: Daytime landline respondents skew older and unrepresentative, so their support for senior-center funding can't be generalized to the public.
questions.theory: Sampling-method bias arises when the means of contacting respondents systematically over- or under-represents groups whose views differ from the population's.

questions.question: A company observes that teams that hold daily stand-up meetings ship products faster. Before crediting the meetings, a skeptic asks whether the faster teams might simply have more experienced members who both prefer stand-ups and work quickly. The skeptic is pointing to the possibility of:
options: A confounding variable, team experience, that could explain both the meeting practice and the faster shipping.
An ambiguous definition of shipping speed.
A bias in how the meetings were scheduled.
A small sample of teams.
questions.explanation: Team experience could independently cause both the preference for stand-ups and the faster work, acting as a confounder.
questions.theory: A confounding variable linked to both the practice and the outcome offers a rival explanation, so it must be controlled before inferring causation.

questions.question: Argument: 'After our town introduced a recycling-education program in schools, household recycling rates rose. The program caused the increase.' Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen this conclusion?
options: The town has a long history of environmental awareness.
The education program was praised by local newspapers.
Children enjoyed participating in the program.
No new recycling bins, curbside pickup changes, or other recycling initiatives were introduced in the town during the same period.
questions.explanation: Confirming no other recycling initiatives coincided rules out alternative causes, supporting that the education program drove the increase.
questions.theory: Eliminating concurrent alternative causes strengthens a causal claim by leaving the proposed cause as the most plausible explanation for the change.

questions.question: A magazine reports that frequent air travelers have higher incomes than infrequent travelers and advises readers to fly more often in order to increase their income. Which of the following best describes the flaw in this advice?
options: It ignores the environmental impact of frequent flying.
It does not specify how often readers should fly.
It mistakes a likely effect of higher income, more travel, for a cause of higher income.
It assumes that all air travel is for business purposes.
questions.explanation: Higher income likely enables more travel, so flying is an effect of wealth, not a cause of it; the advice reverses the causation.
questions.theory: Reverse causation flaws recommend producing the effect to obtain the cause; recognizing which variable plausibly enables the other reveals the error.

questions.question: A researcher claims a tutoring program raises math scores because students who attended scored higher on a year-end test than the school average. Which of the following, if true, most weakens this claim?
options: The tutoring sessions were led by experienced math teachers.
Students were enrolled in the tutoring program only if they had already scored well on a mid-year math test.
The program met twice a week after school.
The year-end test covered the same topics as the tutoring.
questions.explanation: If only students who already scored well were enrolled, their high year-end scores reflect prior ability, not the tutoring's effect.
questions.theory: Selecting participants based on prior high performance guarantees strong outcomes regardless of the intervention, confounding any claim of effectiveness.

questions.question: All certified auditors are licensed accountants. Some licensed accountants are not tax specialists. No tax specialists lack a federal registration. Which statement must be true if every certified auditor is also a tax specialist?
options: No licensed accountant lacks a federal registration.
Every licensed accountant is a certified auditor.
Every certified auditor has a federal registration.
Some licensed accountants who are tax specialists are not certified auditors.
questions.explanation: If every certified auditor is a tax specialist, and all tax specialists have a federal registration, then by transitivity every certified auditor has a federal registration.
questions.theory: In categorical syllogisms, a universal chain A-are-B and B-are-C yields A-are-C by transitivity. Conclusions cannot exceed the scope established by the premises (no existential leaps from purely universal premises).

questions.question: A bridge will collapse only if both its support cables fail. Engineers confirm the bridge has not collapsed. Which conclusion is validly drawn?
options: Both support cables failed.
Neither support cable failed.
At most one support cable failed.
Exactly one support cable failed.
questions.explanation: Collapse requires both cables to fail; since collapse did not occur, it is not the case that both failed, meaning at most one failed.
questions.theory: 'Only if' marks a necessary condition: collapse implies both-fail. Denying the consequent (no collapse) lets you deny the necessary condition (not-both-fail), which is logically equivalent to 'at most one failed.'

questions.question: A study found that towns with more bookstores have higher average reading scores, and concluded that opening bookstores raises reading ability. Which objection most directly identifies the reasoning error?
options: The study should have used a larger sample of towns.
Wealthier towns may both attract bookstores and fund better schools, producing the correlation.
Some residents in high-scoring towns may never visit bookstores.
Reading scores are an imperfect measure of reading ability.
questions.explanation: A common cause (town wealth) could independently produce both more bookstores and higher scores, so the correlation does not establish that bookstores cause the scores.
questions.theory: Correlation does not entail causation; a confounding variable that drives both observed factors is the classic alternative explanation. Identifying a plausible common cause undermines the causal claim without disputing the data.

questions.question: Critic: 'The painting must be authentic, because the gallery that sold it is reputable, and a reputable gallery would never sell a forgery.' Which flaw best describes this argument?
options: It assumes what it sets out to prove by treating the gallery's reputation as guaranteeing authenticity.
It draws a general rule from a single instance.
It confuses a necessary condition with a sufficient one.
It relies on an ambiguous use of the word 'painting.'
questions.explanation: The claim that a reputable gallery never sells forgeries simply restates, as a premise, the very conclusion that this painting is authentic, so the argument assumes what it must prove.
questions.theory: Begging the question occurs when a premise presupposes the truth of the conclusion. The reasoning offers no independent support; it merely repackages the conclusion as an unargued generalization.

questions.question: Principle: A penalty is just only if the offender could reasonably have foreseen that the act was prohibited. Which case, if the principle holds, is most clearly an UNjust penalty?
options: A hiker fined for entering an area whose ban was never posted, announced, or published anywhere.
A merchant fined under a regulation enacted and publicized a year before his violation.
A driver fined for speeding in a clearly posted zone she admits she saw.
A contractor fined after ignoring a written warning about the exact rule he broke.
questions.explanation: The hiker could not reasonably foresee the prohibition because it was never made knowable, so under the principle the penalty fails the foreseeability requirement and is unjust.
questions.theory: Principle-application requires matching the case to the rule's stated condition. A necessary condition (reasonable foreseeability) that is unmet renders the conclusion (just penalty) unavailable, isolating the violating case.

questions.question: Manager: 'Our new training program works. After it launched, the error rate among trainees who completed it dropped by 30 percent.' Which question is most useful for EVALUATING this claim?
options: Did the error rate also drop among comparable employees who did not take the program?
What was the total cost of developing the program?
How long did each training session last?
How many employees enjoyed the training sessions?
questions.explanation: If non-participants experienced the same drop, the decline reflects some broader cause rather than the program, so a comparison group is decisive for assessing the causal claim.
questions.theory: Evaluating a causal claim hinges on ruling out alternative explanations; the most useful question targets a control comparison. A before-after change in one group is uninformative without a baseline for what would have happened anyway.

questions.question: Every member of the committee voted, and every proposal that received a unanimous vote was adopted. Proposal X was not adopted. Which must be true?
options: Proposal X received no votes in favor.
Every committee member voted against Proposal X.
At least one committee member voted against Proposal X.
Proposal X was never put to a vote.
questions.explanation: Since unanimous proposals were all adopted and X was not adopted, X cannot have been unanimous, so at least one member did not vote for it, i.e., voted against it given that all members voted.
questions.theory: Denying the consequent (not adopted) of 'unanimous implies adopted' yields 'not unanimous.' Combined with the premise that all members voted, non-unanimity entails at least one dissenting vote.

questions.question: Argument: 'Nothing that is truly valuable can be bought, since money cannot purchase love or friendship, which are the most valuable things.' Which response best exposes the structural weakness?
options: It overlooks that some people do value money itself.
It assumes friendship is more valuable than love.
It generalizes from a few examples of unbuyable goods to all valuable things.
It uses 'valuable' to mean both 'emotionally precious' and 'commercially priced.'
questions.explanation: The argument moves from the claim that certain especially valuable things cannot be bought to the sweeping claim that nothing valuable can be bought, an unwarranted leap from select cases.
questions.theory: Hasty generalization infers a universal from an unrepresentative sample. That some top-ranked valuables are unpurchasable does not establish that purchasability is incompatible with value in general.

questions.question: Which argument's reasoning is most PARALLEL to: 'If the alarm is working, it sounds when smoke is present. The alarm did not sound, so either it is broken or no smoke was present.'?
options: If the bread is fresh, it is soft. It is stale, so it is not soft.
If the road is icy, cars skid. Cars skidded, so the road is icy.
If the engine is sound, it starts when the key is turned. It did not start, so either the engine is faulty or the key was not turned.
If the plant is healthy, it blooms in spring. It bloomed, so it is healthy.
questions.explanation: Both arguments deny the outcome of a conditional whose trigger has two parts (working/sound and stimulus present), validly concluding that either the device fails or the trigger was absent.
questions.theory: Parallel reasoning matches logical form, not topic. The target denies the consequent of 'P-functional and Q-stimulus implies R,' yielding 'not-P or not-Q'; the correct match shares that exact structure.

questions.question: A pharmaceutical trial reports that 95 percent of patients who took the drug recovered. A skeptic notes this proves little about the drug's efficacy. Which fact, if true, most strongly supports the skeptic?
options: The trial was conducted over only three months.
About 95 percent of untreated patients with this illness recover on their own.
The drug was more expensive than competing treatments.
Some patients reported mild side effects from the drug.
questions.explanation: If the same recovery rate occurs without treatment, the drug adds no detectable benefit, so the high recovery figure does not show the drug is effective.
questions.theory: A treatment's efficacy is measured against the base rate of recovery without it. A high absolute recovery rate is meaningless unless it exceeds what would occur naturally; matching the base rate signals zero effect.

questions.question: Consider: 'All effective leaders are decisive, and Mara is decisive, therefore Mara is an effective leader.' This argument is logically valid.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Being decisive is presented as a necessary feature of effective leaders, not a sufficient one, so a decisive person need not be an effective leader; the argument is invalid.
questions.theory: Affirming a necessary condition does not establish the antecedent. 'All A are B' makes B necessary for A, but possessing B does not guarantee membership in A; this is the fallacy of the undistributed middle.

questions.question: Economist: 'Raising the minimum wage will reduce employment, because higher labor costs force firms to hire fewer workers.' Which assumption is REQUIRED for this conclusion?
options: Minimum-wage workers are the least productive employees.
Consumers will buy fewer goods after the wage increase.
All workers currently earn exactly the minimum wage.
Firms cannot offset higher labor costs through other adjustments such as prices or productivity.
questions.explanation: The conclusion holds only if firms have no alternative response to higher costs besides cutting jobs; if they can adjust elsewhere, employment need not fall.
questions.theory: A required assumption is one whose negation breaks the argument. If firms could absorb costs via prices or efficiency, the inference from higher labor costs to fewer hires collapses, so ruling that out is necessary.

questions.question: Whenever the river floods, the lowland fields are damaged. The lowland fields were damaged this year. A farmer concludes the river must have flooded. The farmer's reasoning is flawed because:
options: damage to the fields is a necessary, not sufficient, condition for flooding.
the river floods every year regardless of the fields.
the fields might not have been damaged at all.
other causes, such as a storm, could have damaged the fields without a flood.
questions.explanation: Flooding is sufficient for damage but not the only possible cause, so observing damage does not establish that flooding specifically occurred.
questions.theory: Affirming the consequent: from 'flood implies damage' and 'damage,' one cannot infer 'flood.' The conditional leaves open other sufficient causes of the consequent, making the inference invalid.

questions.question: To qualify for the scholarship, an applicant must either have a 3.8 GPA or have won a national competition. Jonah did not win any national competition. Which additional fact guarantees Jonah qualifies?
options: Jonah has a 3.7 GPA and strong recommendations.
Jonah won a regional, but not national, competition.
Jonah applied before the deadline with a complete file.
Jonah has a 3.8 GPA.
questions.explanation: With the national-competition path closed, the remaining sufficient route is the 3.8 GPA, which Jonah meets, so qualification is guaranteed.
questions.theory: A disjunctive requirement is satisfied by meeting either disjunct. Eliminating one disjunct (no national win) makes the other (3.8 GPA) the operative sufficient condition; meeting it secures the conclusion.

questions.question: Advocate: 'We should ban all advertising aimed at children, because children cannot critically evaluate persuasive messages, and exposing people to messages they cannot evaluate is manipulative.' Which principle, if accepted, most strengthens this argument?
options: It is wrong to subject anyone to influence they are incapable of critically assessing.
Most advertisements contain some factual information.
Advertising is generally beneficial to economic growth.
Children should be supervised by their parents at all times.
questions.explanation: The stated principle bridges the premise about children's inability to evaluate messages to the conclusion that such advertising should be banned by labeling the practice impermissible.
questions.theory: A strengthening principle supplies a general rule that licenses the move from the cited facts to the normative conclusion. The right principle generalizes exactly the wrong-making feature the argument relies upon.

questions.question: Some physicists are not theorists. All theorists publish frequently. Everyone who publishes frequently is well-funded. Which conclusion is validly supported?
options: Some physicists are not well-funded.
Some well-funded people are theorists.
All physicists are well-funded.
No physicist publishes frequently.
questions.explanation: Theorists exist within the premises, all theorists publish frequently, and all who publish frequently are well-funded, so some well-funded people are theorists.
questions.theory: Chaining universals (theorist to publish to well-funded) preserves the subject across the chain. Since 'all theorists' presupposes theorists in the domain, the existential conclusion about well-funded theorists follows; claims about physicists do not.

questions.question: Pollster: 'A survey of visitors to our investment website found that 80 percent expect stocks to rise, so most people are optimistic about the market.' The reasoning is most vulnerable to which criticism?
options: The sample is drawn from a group likely to be unusually optimistic about stocks.
The survey did not ask about bond markets.
Eighty percent is not a large enough majority to support the claim.
Optimism cannot be measured by a single survey question.
questions.explanation: Visitors to an investment website are not representative of people in general; they self-select for market enthusiasm, so their views cannot ground a claim about most people.
questions.theory: Sampling bias arises when the surveyed group differs systematically from the population of interest. A self-selected, non-random sample cannot support a generalization to the broader population regardless of the percentage observed.

questions.question: If a contract is valid, then it is enforceable. If a contract is enforceable, then a court will uphold it. A court refused to uphold this contract. Which conclusion follows?
options: The contract is not valid.
The contract is enforceable.
The contract is enforceable but invalid.
The contract is valid but unenforceable.
questions.explanation: Chaining the conditionals gives 'valid implies upheld'; since the court did not uphold it, the contract cannot be valid.
questions.theory: Hypothetical syllogism links 'valid implies enforceable' and 'enforceable implies upheld' into 'valid implies upheld.' Denying the final consequent (not upheld) lets you deny the antecedent (not valid) by modus tollens.

questions.question: Columnist: 'Free speech must have limits, because no freedom is absolute, and anything that is not absolute must have limits.' A logician objects that the argument trades on two senses of a key term. Which term, and how?
options: 'Speech,' meaning both spoken words and expression generally.
'Limits,' meaning both legal boundaries and physical edges.
'Absolute,' meaning both 'without exception' in the first use and 'morally perfect' in the second.
'Free,' meaning both unpriced and unrestricted.
questions.explanation: If 'absolute' shifts from 'exceptionless' to another sense between premises, the conclusion does not follow; the objection identifies this equivocation on 'absolute.'
questions.theory: Equivocation is a fallacy in which a term changes meaning across an argument, so the premises only appear to connect. Detecting it requires checking whether each occurrence of the pivotal word carries a consistent sense.

questions.question: All licensed pilots have passed a vision test. Some who passed the vision test are colorblind. No colorblind person may fly at night. Which must be true?
options: All who passed the vision test are licensed pilots.
Some licensed pilots are colorblind.
Some who passed the vision test may not fly at night.
No licensed pilot may fly at night.
questions.explanation: Some vision-test passers are colorblind, and no colorblind person may fly at night, so some vision-test passers may not fly at night.
questions.theory: An existential premise ('some passers are colorblind') combined with a universal restriction on that subset yields an existential conclusion about the larger set. Claims about pilots overreach, since the colorblind passers need not be pilots.

questions.question: Researcher: 'Our diagnostic test is 99 percent accurate, so a person who tests positive almost certainly has the disease.' Even granting the accuracy figure, this conclusion can fail because:
options: when the disease is very rare, most positives can still be false positives.
the test may be expensive to administer widely.
accuracy and precision are different statistical concepts.
99 percent accuracy is too low for medical use.
questions.explanation: If the disease is rare, the small false-positive rate applied to the large healthy population can outnumber true positives, so a positive result need not mean the person is likely diseased.
questions.theory: The base-rate fallacy ignores prior prevalence when interpreting test results. Posterior probability depends on the disease's rarity; with a low base rate, even a highly accurate test produces many false positives among the positives.

questions.question: Which argument is most PARALLEL in reasoning to: 'Every senator who supported the bill received donor funding; Reyes received no donor funding; therefore Reyes did not support the bill.'?
options: Every car that passed inspection had new brakes; this car has new brakes; therefore it passed inspection.
Every dog that was trained obeys commands; this dog obeys commands; therefore it was trained.
Every student who graduated completed the thesis; Lana did not complete the thesis; therefore Lana did not graduate.
Every tree that survived was watered; this tree was watered; therefore it survived.
questions.explanation: Both validly deny the antecedent's necessary condition: lacking the required feature (donor funding / completed thesis) entails not having the property (supported / graduated) via modus tollens.
questions.theory: Valid parallel reasoning shares form. The target is 'all A are B; not-B(x); so not-A(x)' (modus tollens); the matching choice denies the necessary condition to deny membership, unlike the affirming-the-consequent distractors.

questions.question: Debater: 'My opponent claims the policy will help the poor, but he is a wealthy man who has never experienced poverty, so his claim is false.' The reasoning is flawed primarily because:
options: it assumes the policy will definitely fail.
it generalizes from one wealthy person to all wealthy people.
it attacks the opponent's circumstances rather than the merits of the claim.
it confuses the policy's intent with its effect.
questions.explanation: The argument rejects the claim based on who is making it rather than on any evidence about the policy, which does not bear on whether the claim is true.
questions.theory: An ad hominem (circumstantial) fallacy dismisses a position by appealing to the arguer's traits or situation. The truth of a claim is independent of the speaker's wealth or experience; relevance to the claim itself is what matters.

questions.question: No counterfeit coins are accepted by the machine. All coins minted before 1990 are accepted by the machine. Therefore, no coin minted before 1990 is counterfeit. Which assumption makes this conclusion follow validly?
options: The machine rejects all coins minted after 1990.
All genuine coins are accepted by the machine.
Some counterfeit coins were minted before 1990.
The machine accepts only genuine coins.
questions.explanation: Given that pre-1990 coins are all accepted, concluding they are not counterfeit requires that everything accepted is genuine, i.e., the machine accepts only genuine coins.
questions.theory: A valid categorical conclusion may need a suppressed premise to close the chain. To get 'pre-1990 implies not-counterfeit' from 'pre-1990 implies accepted,' you need 'accepted implies genuine,' which the missing premise supplies.

questions.question: Health official: 'More people die in hospitals than at rock concerts, so hospitals are more dangerous than rock concerts.' The reasoning is flawed because it ignores that:
options: hospitals provide medical care that concerts do not.
rock concerts are also attended by healthy people.
some deaths at concerts go unrecorded.
far more people, many already gravely ill, are present in hospitals.
questions.explanation: Hospitals serve vastly more people, many already critically ill, so a higher raw death count reflects exposure and prior risk rather than greater danger per person.
questions.theory: Comparing absolute counts without adjusting for the size and risk profile of the populations is a base-rate error. Danger should be assessed as a rate relative to exposure, not by unadjusted totals.

questions.question: If the thermostat is set above 70 degrees, the heater runs. The heater is running. Which of the following, if added, would let us validly conclude the thermostat is set above 70 degrees?
options: The heater runs only if the thermostat is set above 70 degrees.
The thermostat is sometimes set above 70 degrees.
When the heater runs, the room warms.
The heater runs whenever the thermostat is set above 70 degrees.
questions.explanation: Adding that the heater runs only if the thermostat is above 70 makes that setting necessary for the heater running, so the running heater entails the setting.
questions.theory: To infer a cause from an effect, you need the effect to be sufficient for the cause, i.e., an 'only if' converse. 'P implies Q' plus Q does not give P, but 'Q only if P' (Q implies P) does.

questions.question: Editor: 'We must publish the exposé. Either we publish it or the corruption continues unchecked, and we cannot allow corruption to continue unchecked.' The argument's validity depends on assuming that:
options: readers will respond strongly to the exposé.
publishing the exposé is the only way to check the corruption.
the corruption is more serious than other current scandals.
the exposé has been thoroughly fact-checked.
questions.explanation: The disjunction 'publish or corruption continues' is only exhaustive if no other means could check the corruption, so the argument assumes publishing is the sole remedy.
questions.theory: A disjunctive syllogism is valid only if the disjunction is genuinely exhaustive. A false dilemma arises when unstated alternatives exist; the argument must assume away those alternatives for the inference to hold.

questions.question: Principle: One is morally responsible for a harm only if one could have acted otherwise. Which scenario, under this principle, most clearly relieves the agent of responsibility for a harm?
options: A cashier who, physically restrained by robbers, could not trigger the alarm.
A driver who sped because he was running late by choice.
A guard who slept through his shift, having chosen to stay up late.
A clerk who ignored a warning he could easily have heeded.
