questions.explanation: The restrained cashier literally could not have acted otherwise, so the principle's necessary condition for responsibility is unmet and he is not responsible.
questions.theory: Applying a principle means testing whether its necessary condition holds. 'Could have done otherwise' fails only under genuine inability; cases involving prior free choices do not remove the capacity to act differently.

questions.question: A company notes that its highest-performing salespeople all use the premium CRM software, and concludes the software causes high performance. Which alternative explanation most undermines this?
options: Top performers are given premium licenses as a reward for already excelling.
The software is updated frequently by its vendor.
The premium software has more features than the basic version.
Some low performers also use the premium software occasionally.
questions.explanation: If high performance leads to receiving the software rather than the reverse, the correlation reflects reversed causation and does not show the software produces performance.
questions.theory: Reverse causation is a key rival to a causal claim: the supposed effect may actually cause the supposed cause. Establishing direction requires showing the cause precedes and is not merely a consequence of the effect.

questions.question: All members of the orchestra can read music. Anyone who can read music can sight-sing. Some who can sight-sing have perfect pitch. Which statement is NOT supported by these premises?
options: All orchestra members can sight-sing.
Anyone who cannot sight-sing is not an orchestra member.
Some people with perfect pitch can sight-sing.
All orchestra members have perfect pitch.
questions.explanation: Perfect pitch is attributed only to some sight-singers, so nothing forces orchestra members to have it; that claim is unsupported.
questions.theory: An existential premise ('some sight-singers have perfect pitch') cannot yield a universal conclusion about a different group. The chain supports universal claims down to sight-singing but stops short of distributing perfect pitch to all members.

questions.question: Scientist: 'This vaccine is safe. In our trial of 10,000 people, no serious adverse events occurred.' Which consideration most limits the strength of this safety conclusion?
options: Adverse events rarer than one in 10,000 could easily be missed by a trial this size.
The trial did not measure the vaccine's effectiveness.
The participants were not paid for their time.
The vaccine was developed by a private company.
questions.explanation: A sample of 10,000 cannot reliably detect adverse events that occur less frequently than that, so the absence of events in the trial does not establish overall safety.
questions.theory: Sample size bounds the rarity of effects that can be detected. Absence of evidence for very rare events in a limited sample is not strong evidence of their absence; the conclusion outruns what the data can support.

questions.question: Consider: 'If it rained, the ground is wet. The ground is wet. Therefore it rained.' This argument commits the fallacy of affirming the consequent.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: The argument affirms the consequent (wet ground) and infers the antecedent (rain), but the ground could be wet for other reasons, so it is the fallacy named.
questions.theory: Affirming the consequent has the invalid form 'P implies Q; Q; therefore P.' Because Q can have causes other than P, the truth of Q does not establish P, making the inference fallacious.

questions.question: Politician: 'My tax plan will work, because economists who oppose it are simply protecting their own elite interests.' This response is flawed because it:
options: relies on a sample of economists too small to be representative.
assumes the plan has already been implemented.
dismisses the critics' arguments by impugning their motives rather than addressing the arguments.
confuses the plan's goals with its likely outcomes.
questions.explanation: The politician sidesteps the substance of the critics' objections by questioning their motives, which does nothing to show the objections are mistaken.
questions.theory: This is a circumstantial ad hominem (poisoning the well): attacking motives in place of refuting claims. Even self-interested critics may offer valid arguments; the arguments must be addressed on their merits.

questions.question: Either the witness lied or the defendant was present at the scene. If the defendant was present, the security footage would show her. The footage does not show her. Which conclusion follows?
options: The security footage malfunctioned.
The witness lied.
The witness told the truth.
The defendant was present at the scene.
questions.explanation: Since the footage would show the defendant if present but does not, she was not present, so by the disjunction the witness lied.
questions.theory: This combines modus tollens with disjunctive syllogism: deny the consequent to get 'not present,' then eliminate that disjunct to affirm the remaining one. Both steps are valid forms chained together.

questions.question: Which argument's flaw is most SIMILAR to: 'Most accidents happen within five miles of home, so driving far from home is safer than driving nearby.'?
options: Most fatal falls occur on level ground, so climbing is safer than walking.
Most lottery winners bought tickets, so buying tickets makes winning likely.
Most reading happens indoors, so reading outdoors must improve eyesight.
Most students pass the exam, so the exam is too easy.
questions.explanation: Both ignore that the bulk of activity (driving near home / walking on level ground) occurs in the supposedly 'dangerous' setting, so raw frequency does not indicate higher per-instance risk.
questions.theory: This flaw confuses event frequency with rate per exposure, neglecting how much activity occurs in each condition. The parallel must share that exposure-neglect structure, not merely sound superficially similar.

questions.question: To be admitted, a student must submit transcripts and pass an interview. Failing either disqualifies the applicant. Priya passed her interview. Which conclusion is warranted?
options: Priya will be admitted.
Priya failed to meet at least one requirement.
If Priya did not submit transcripts, she will not be admitted.
Priya submitted her transcripts.
questions.explanation: Both conditions are necessary; passing the interview is not enough, so if Priya lacks transcripts she fails a requirement and will not be admitted.
questions.theory: When admission requires the conjunction of two necessary conditions, satisfying one leaves the outcome open. The only secure inference is conditional: absence of the other necessary condition guarantees the negative result.

questions.question: Critic: 'This novel is the greatest of the decade. After all, no other novel of the decade is as great as this one.' The argument is defective because it:
options: merely restates its conclusion in different words as if it were evidence.
compares the novel only to others from the same decade.
fails to define what makes a novel great.
relies on the subjective opinion of a single critic.
questions.explanation: Saying no other novel is as great is just a paraphrase of the claim that it is the greatest, so the premise repeats the conclusion rather than supporting it.
questions.theory: Circular reasoning offers a premise that is logically equivalent to the conclusion. No independent ground is given; the appearance of support comes only from rewording the same assertion.

questions.question: A manager observes that departments with more meetings have lower productivity and concludes meetings reduce productivity. Which finding would most strengthen this causal conclusion?
options: Productivity is difficult to measure precisely.
High-meeting departments also tend to have larger budgets.
When a department experimentally cut its meetings in half, its productivity rose.
Employees generally dislike attending meetings.
questions.explanation: A controlled reduction in meetings followed by a productivity increase provides experimental evidence that meetings depress productivity, directly supporting the causal direction.
questions.theory: Manipulating the proposed cause and observing the predicted change in the effect is the strongest non-confounded support for causation. Intervention evidence outranks mere correlation by addressing direction and confounders.

questions.question: All who attended the gala donated. Some donors are board members. No board member is an employee. Which must be true?
options: All board members attended the gala.
No attendee is an employee.
Some donors are not employees.
Some attendees are not employees.
questions.explanation: Some donors are board members, and no board member is an employee, so those donors are not employees, making 'some donors are not employees' true.
questions.theory: From 'some D are B' and 'no B are E' we get 'some D are not E' by a valid syllogism. The conclusion about donors is secure, while claims that all attendees avoid employee status overreach the existential premise.

questions.question: Advertiser: 'Nine out of ten dentists surveyed recommend our toothpaste, so it is the best toothpaste available.' Which fact, if true, most undermines the inference to 'best'?
options: The tenth dentist recommended a different brand.
The toothpaste contains a common fluoride compound.
The survey included dentists from several countries.
Dentists were asked only whether the toothpaste was acceptable, not whether it was best.
questions.explanation: If dentists merely judged the toothpaste acceptable rather than superior, their recommendations cannot support the stronger claim that it is the best available.
questions.theory: An equivocation-like gap arises when the evidence ('acceptable') is weaker than the conclusion ('best'). Recommendation as adequate does not entail ranking first; the inference inflates the strength of the underlying data.

questions.question: If the company is solvent, it pays dividends. If it pays dividends, shareholders are satisfied. Shareholders are not satisfied. Additionally, the company is either solvent or being audited. Which follows?
options: The company pays dividends.
The company is solvent.
The company is being audited.
Shareholders are satisfied after all.
questions.explanation: Chaining gives 'solvent implies satisfied'; since shareholders are not satisfied, the company is not solvent, so by the disjunction it is being audited.
questions.theory: Hypothetical syllogism yields 'solvent implies satisfied'; modus tollens then gives 'not solvent'; disjunctive syllogism on 'solvent or audited' yields 'audited.' Three valid forms compose into one conclusion.

questions.question: Sociologist: 'Communities that ban alcohol have higher rates of secret drinking; therefore prohibition increases alcohol abuse.' Which assumption is the argument most dependent on?
options: Alcohol abuse is harmful to communities.
Prohibition is difficult to enforce in practice.
Secret drinking is an accurate indicator of overall alcohol abuse.
Most residents of these communities support the ban.
questions.explanation: The conclusion about 'alcohol abuse' rests on treating secret drinking as a valid measure of abuse; if it is not, the higher secret-drinking rate says nothing about abuse levels.
questions.theory: Arguments that infer a broad outcome from a narrow proxy depend on the proxy faithfully representing the outcome. If the measured variable does not track the conclusion's variable, the inference is unfounded.

questions.question: Which argument is most PARALLEL to: 'No genuine masterpiece is ever forgotten; this work has been forgotten; so it is not a genuine masterpiece.'?
options: No healthy plant wilts; this plant is healthy; so it will not wilt.
No safe medication causes harm; this medication is safe; so it causes no harm.
No reliable witness ever contradicts the evidence; this witness contradicted the evidence; so this witness is not reliable.
No expensive car is unreliable; this car is reliable; so it is expensive.
questions.explanation: Both have the valid form 'no A is B; x is B; so x is not A,' denying category membership because the subject has the excluded property.
questions.theory: The form 'no A are B; Bx; therefore not-Ax' is valid (a categorical modus tollens). The matching argument applies the excluded predicate to the subject to deny membership, unlike the converse-error distractors.

questions.question: Two reviewers disagree: Reviewer A says the experiment is flawed because the sample was not randomized; Reviewer B says randomization was unnecessary because the groups were already similar in all relevant respects. Reviewer B's defense would be most weakened by showing that:
options: the sample size was relatively small.
the experiment was costly to run.
an unmeasured variable that affects the outcome differed between the groups.
randomization is a standard scientific practice.
questions.explanation: Reviewer B relies on the groups being similar in all relevant respects; an unmeasured outcome-affecting difference shows they were not, defeating that defense.
questions.theory: Randomization guards against unmeasured confounders. A claim that it was unnecessary because groups were 'already similar' fails if a hidden relevant difference exists, since only randomization can balance unknown variables in expectation.

questions.question: Lawmaker: 'If we permit this minor exception to the speech code, we will inevitably end up banning all controversial speech, so we must reject the exception.' Absent further support, this reasoning is flawed because it:
options: defines 'controversial speech' too narrowly.
attacks the character of those proposing the exception.
relies on the testimony of an interested party.
assumes without justification that one small step must lead to an extreme outcome.
questions.explanation: The argument asserts that a minor exception will inevitably cascade into an extreme result without showing why each step must follow, which is the unsupported part.
questions.theory: A slippery-slope fallacy claims an initial action triggers an inevitable chain to a dire end without establishing the causal links. The reasoning fails unless each transition is independently justified.

questions.question: Every theorem in the textbook is proved rigorously. Some rigorously proved claims rely on the axiom of choice. Nothing relying on the axiom of choice is constructive. Which conclusion is validly supported?
options: All constructive claims are in the textbook.
Every theorem in the textbook relies on the axiom of choice.
No theorem in the textbook is constructive.
Some rigorously proved claims are not constructive.
questions.explanation: Some rigorously proved claims use the axiom of choice, and nothing using it is constructive, so some rigorously proved claims are not constructive.
questions.theory: From 'some R are C-axiom' and 'no C-axiom are constructive,' it follows that 'some R are not constructive.' The existential premise blocks any universal conclusion about all textbook theorems.

questions.question: Consider: 'Whoever is qualified is hired, and Sam was hired, so Sam is qualified.' Given only this, the conclusion is logically guaranteed.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: The premise makes qualification sufficient for being hired, not necessary, so Sam could have been hired without being qualified; the conclusion is not guaranteed.
questions.theory: 'Whoever is qualified is hired' means qualified implies hired. Affirming the consequent (hired) does not yield the antecedent (qualified), since other paths to being hired may exist.

questions.question: A historian argues: 'The treaty caused the war's end, because the war ended shortly after the treaty was signed.' Which question most directly tests whether this is a causal relationship rather than mere sequence?
options: Were the military and economic conditions already forcing the war to end regardless of the treaty?
Was the treaty signed by all parties to the conflict?
Did the treaty contain provisions for postwar reconstruction?
Was the treaty longer than previous agreements?
questions.explanation: If the war was already ending due to other forces, the treaty merely preceded the end rather than causing it, so this question separates causation from coincidence.
questions.theory: Post hoc reasoning infers causation from temporal succession alone. The decisive test is whether the effect would have occurred anyway; identifying independent sufficient causes distinguishes genuine cause from mere sequence.

questions.question: Manager: 'Productivity rose 20 percent after we adopted the four-day week, proving shorter weeks boost output. We should cut to three days for even greater gains.' Which assumption is most necessary for the recommendation to extend to three days?
options: Employees prefer fewer workdays to more pay.
The relationship between fewer workdays and higher output continues to hold as days are reduced further.
Productivity is the only goal worth pursuing.
The four-day week was popular among managers as well.
questions.explanation: Recommending three days assumes the gains from cutting days keep accruing rather than leveling off or reversing, so the trend must be presumed to continue beyond the tested point.
questions.theory: Extrapolating a recommendation beyond observed data requires assuming the relationship remains monotonic outside the tested range. Effects observed at one level need not persist at more extreme levels; that continuity is the load-bearing assumption.
